hook.text: In 400 BC, Democritus proposed that all matter is made of tiny, indivisible particles he called 'atomos.'

buildup.text: He reasoned that if you kept cutting matter in half, you'd eventually reach a piece that couldn't be cut further.

discovery.text: He claimed atoms differ in shape, size, and arrangement—and these differences explain all material properties.

twist.text: Aristotle rejected atoms and proposed continuous matter instead. His authority buried atomism for 2,000 years.

climax.text: Democritus was right—atoms exist. But it took until 1803 for science to prove what philosophy had guessed.

punchline.text: Uncuttable particles, 400 BC. Right idea, 2,200 years too early.

quiz.question: Why was Democritus's atomic theory ignored for 2,000 years?
options: Aristotle's rival theory of continuous matter dominated Western thought
Democritus was exiled and his writings were destroyed
Atomic theory was considered blasphemous by Greek religion

hook.text: Aristotle argued against atoms. He claimed matter is continuous and made of four elements—earth, water, air, fire.

buildup.text: He believed a vacuum (empty space between atoms) was impossible. 'Nature abhors a vacuum,' he declared.

discovery.text: His four-element system explained observations: wood burns (contains fire), ice melts (contains water).

twist.text: Aristotle's framework was intuitive and wrong. But it dominated science until the 1600s—a 2,000-year detour.

climax.text: The irony: Aristotle championed observation and logic, but his authority prevented others from questioning him.

punchline.text: Intuitive, logical, completely wrong. Authority can block truth for millennia.

quiz.question: What was Aristotle's main argument against atoms?
options: He believed matter was continuous and a vacuum between atoms was impossible
He thought atoms were too small to affect observable properties
He argued that only gods could create indivisible particles

hook.text: In 50 BC, the Roman poet Lucretius wrote a poem about atoms that was lost for 1,400 years—then changed the world.

buildup.text: 'De Rerum Natura' (On the Nature of Things) explained Epicurean atomic theory in 7,400 lines of Latin verse.

discovery.text: Lucretius argued that atoms move, collide, and combine—explaining everything from rain to consciousness.

twist.text: A single manuscript survived in a German monastery. Found in 1417, it sparked the Renaissance revival of atomism.

climax.text: Galileo, Newton, and Einstein all read Lucretius. His poem planted the seed that grew into modern physics.

punchline.text: A poem about atoms, lost and found. One manuscript helped restart science.

quiz.question: How did Lucretius's poem influence the scientific revolution?
options: Its rediscovery in 1417 revived atomic theory and inspired later scientists
It contained mathematical proofs of atomic weights
It predicted the discovery of electrons and protons

hook.text: Around 600 BC—before Democritus—the Indian philosopher Kanada proposed that all matter is made of 'paramanu.'

buildup.text: Kanada taught that paramanu are eternal, indestructible, and combine in pairs and triplets to form visible matter.

discovery.text: He classified matter into nine types: earth, water, fire, air, space, time, direction, mind, and soul.

twist.text: His Vaisheshika school described atoms combining through inherent properties—an early concept of chemical bonding.

climax.text: Kanada's ideas developed independently from Greek atomism. Two civilizations reached the same insight separately.

punchline.text: Two continents, same idea: matter has smallest parts. Convergent thinking.

quiz.question: What is significant about Kanada's atomic theory?
options: It developed independently from Greek atomism, showing convergent thinking
It was mathematically more precise than Democritus's version
It correctly predicted the number of elements

hook.text: In 1827, Robert Brown watched pollen grains jiggle in water. He couldn't explain why—but Einstein could.

buildup.text: Brown saw tiny pollen particles moving randomly in water under a microscope. No force seemed to push them.

discovery.text: In 1905, Einstein proved the jiggling was caused by invisible water molecules bombarding the pollen from all sides.

twist.text: Many scientists still doubted atoms existed in 1905. Einstein's math showed the jiggle matched atomic theory perfectly.

climax.text: Jean Perrin confirmed Einstein's predictions experimentally in 1908—and won the Nobel Prize for proving atoms are real.

punchline.text: Pollen jiggles, math explains why. Invisible molecules pushing visible grains.

quiz.question: How did Einstein explain Brownian motion?
options: Invisible water molecules randomly bombard visible particles from all sides
Tiny electrical charges in water repel the pollen
Heat convection currents in the water push particles around

hook.text: In 1803, John Dalton proposed that each element is made of unique atoms with a specific weight. Modern chemistry began.

buildup.text: Dalton noticed that elements combine in fixed ratios by weight—hydrogen and oxygen always combine as 1:8.

discovery.text: He concluded: atoms of each element are identical, atoms of different elements differ in weight and properties.

twist.text: Dalton was colorblind—literally. He studied his own condition and 'daltonism' became a word for colorblindness.

climax.text: His atomic weights were often wrong, but the concept was revolutionary. Chemistry became quantitative, not qualitative.

punchline.text: Fixed ratios mean fixed atoms. Dalton turned chemistry into a counting science.

quiz.question: What key observation led Dalton to propose atomic theory?
options: Elements combine in fixed ratios by weight
All gases expand at the same rate when heated
Elements have unique colors when burned

hook.text: One mole of any substance contains exactly 602,214,076,000,000,000,000,000 particles. That's Avogadro's number.

buildup.text: In 1811, Avogadro proposed that equal volumes of gas at the same temperature and pressure hold equal numbers of particles.

discovery.text: This meant you could count atoms by weighing them. One mole of carbon weighs 12 grams and contains 6.022 × 10²³ atoms.

twist.text: Avogadro never knew his number. It was calculated 50 years after his death by Johann Loschmidt.

climax.text: If you counted one atom per second, it would take 19 quadrillion years to count one mole. That's a big number.

punchline.text: A bridge between the atomic and human scale. Weigh to count.

quiz.question: What is Avogadro's number approximately?
options: 6.022 × 10²³ particles per mole
3.14 × 10¹⁵ particles per gram
9.8 × 10¹⁸ particles per liter

hook.text: In 1869, Mendeleev arranged 63 elements by weight and left gaps—predicting elements that hadn't been found yet.

buildup.text: He noticed that properties repeat periodically when elements are ordered by atomic weight.

discovery.text: He left gaps for three undiscovered elements, predicting their atomic weights and chemical properties.

twist.text: Within 15 years, all three were found: gallium, scandium, and germanium—matching his predictions almost exactly.

climax.text: The periodic table became chemistry's most powerful organizing tool—and Mendeleev its greatest prophet.

punchline.text: Gaps in a table that predicted the future. Pattern recognition at its finest.

quiz.question: How did Mendeleev validate his periodic table?
options: He predicted properties of undiscovered elements that were later found to match
He synthesized three new elements in his laboratory
He proved that all elements are made of the same atom

hook.text: Joseph Gay-Lussac discovered that gases combine in simple whole-number volume ratios. This puzzled everyone.

buildup.text: Two volumes of hydrogen combine with one volume of oxygen to make two volumes of water vapor. Always 2:1:2.

discovery.text: Simple ratios suggested that gases contain equal numbers of particles in equal volumes—but Dalton disagreed.

twist.text: Dalton couldn't accept that some elements existed as two-atom molecules (H₂, O₂). He thought atoms were always single.

climax.text: Avogadro solved it: gases form diatomic molecules. Two hydrogen molecules plus one oxygen molecule makes two water molecules.

punchline.text: Simple volume ratios, diatomic molecules. Gases were counting atoms for us.

quiz.question: What did Gay-Lussac's law reveal about how gases combine?
options: They react in simple whole-number volume ratios
Heavier gases always sink below lighter gases
Temperature doesn't affect gas reactions

hook.text: A mole isn't just a number—it's chemistry's bridge between individual atoms and weighable, measurable amounts.

buildup.text: One atom of carbon weighs 2 × 10⁻²³ grams—far too small to weigh. But 6.022 × 10²³ atoms weigh exactly 12 grams.

discovery.text: The mole lets chemists convert between atoms and grams. If a reaction needs 2 moles of hydrogen, weigh out 4 grams.

twist.text: October 23 (10/23) is Mole Day, celebrating Avogadro's number. Chemistry students celebrate from 6:02 AM to 6:02 PM.

climax.text: In 2019, the mole was redefined: exactly 6.02214076 × 10²³ entities. No more uncertainty in the definition.

punchline.text: Count by weighing, weigh by counting. The mole connects two scales.

quiz.question: Why is the mole concept essential to chemistry?
options: It lets chemists convert between individual atoms and measurable grams
It defines the maximum number of elements possible
It measures the speed of chemical reactions

hook.text: In 1897, J.J. Thomson found that atoms aren't indivisible. They contain tiny negative particles—electrons.

buildup.text: He shot cathode rays through electric and magnetic fields. The rays bent, proving they were charged particles.

discovery.text: He calculated the charge-to-mass ratio and found these particles were 1,800 times lighter than hydrogen atoms.

twist.text: He proposed the 'plum pudding model': electrons embedded in a positive sphere, like raisins in a pudding.

climax.text: Thomson was wrong about the model—but right about the electron. He won the Nobel Prize in 1906.

punchline.text: Atoms have parts. The first subatomic particle was found in a glass tube.

quiz.question: How did Thomson discover the electron?
options: He showed cathode rays were tiny charged particles deflected by electric fields
He split atoms using radioactive bombardment
He observed electrons orbiting atoms under a microscope

hook.text: Rutherford fired alpha particles at gold foil. Most passed through—but some bounced straight back. He was stunned.

buildup.text: If Thomson's plum pudding model was right, all particles should pass through the diffuse positive charge.

discovery.text: One in 8,000 particles bounced back. Rutherford concluded all positive charge was concentrated in a tiny, dense nucleus.

twist.text: He compared it to firing artillery at tissue paper and having shells bounce back. The atom was mostly empty space.

climax.text: The nucleus contains 99.97% of the atom's mass but only 1/100,000th of its volume. Atoms are almost entirely nothing.

punchline.text: Most passed through, a few bounced back. A tiny dense core in a cloud of nothing.

quiz.question: What did Rutherford conclude from alpha particles bouncing back?
options: The atom's positive charge is concentrated in a tiny, dense nucleus
Atoms are solid spheres with no internal structure
Electrons orbit in fixed rings around the nucleus

hook.text: For 20 years, physicists knew atoms were too heavy for just protons and electrons. Something was missing.

buildup.text: Helium has an atomic mass of 4 but only 2 protons. What accounts for the other half of its mass?

discovery.text: In 1932, James Chadwick bombarded beryllium with alpha particles and detected a new particle with no charge—the neutron.

twist.text: Neutrons have almost the same mass as protons. They hold the nucleus together despite protons repelling each other.

climax.text: The neutron made nuclear fission possible. Without charge, neutrons penetrate nuclei easily—triggering chain reactions.

punchline.text: No charge, nearly invisible. The neutral particle that completed the atom.

quiz.question: Why was discovering the neutron so important?
options: Neutrons have no charge, letting them penetrate nuclei and trigger fission
Neutrons proved that atoms can be destroyed
Neutrons showed that protons are not fundamental particles

hook.text: Carbon-12 and Carbon-14 are the same element with the same chemistry—but different masses. They're isotopes.

buildup.text: Isotopes have the same number of protons (same element) but different numbers of neutrons (different mass).

discovery.text: Frederick Soddy discovered isotopes in 1913 while studying radioactive decay chains of thorium and uranium.

twist.text: Carbon-14 is radioactive with a half-life of 5,730 years. Archaeologists use it to date ancient objects up to 50,000 years old.

climax.text: Hydrogen has three isotopes: protium (0 neutrons), deuterium (1 neutron), and tritium (2 neutrons, radioactive).

punchline.text: Same protons, different neutrons. Same chemistry, different stability.

quiz.question: What makes two atoms isotopes of the same element?
options: Same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons
Same number of electrons but different protons
Same atomic mass but different chemical properties

hook.text: Protons are all positively charged. They should repel each other and blow the nucleus apart. Something holds them together.

buildup.text: Electromagnetic repulsion between protons is enormous at nuclear distances. A new force must overpower it.

discovery.text: The strong nuclear force is 100 times stronger than electromagnetism—but only works at distances under 10⁻¹⁵ meters.

twist.text: Neutrons help by adding strong force without electromagnetic repulsion. Too few neutrons and the nucleus flies apart.

climax.text: The strong force is carried by gluons, which bind quarks inside protons and neutrons. It's the universe's strongest force.

punchline.text: The strongest force in nature, invisible beyond a femtometer. It holds matter together.

quiz.question: Why don't the positively charged protons in a nucleus fly apart?
options: The strong nuclear force is 100 times stronger than electromagnetic repulsion at short range
Electrons between protons neutralize the charge
Neutrons create a magnetic shield around the protons

hook.text: In 1913, Niels Bohr proposed that electrons orbit the nucleus in fixed energy levels—like planets around a sun.

buildup.text: Classical physics predicted orbiting electrons should radiate energy and spiral into the nucleus. Atoms should collapse.

discovery.text: Bohr said electrons can only exist at certain distances. They jump between levels by absorbing or emitting exact energy.

twist.text: This explained hydrogen's spectral lines perfectly—each color corresponds to an electron jumping between specific levels.

climax.text: Bohr's model was wrong for complex atoms. But it introduced quantization to chemistry—a revolutionary idea.

punchline.text: Fixed orbits, quantum jumps, no spiraling in. A wrong model that changed everything.

quiz.question: What revolutionary idea did Bohr introduce to atomic theory?
options: Electrons exist only in fixed energy levels and jump between them
Electrons orbit randomly at any distance from the nucleus
Electrons are stationary and don't orbit at all

hook.text: Electrons don't orbit like planets. They exist as probability clouds—you can never know exactly where one is.

buildup.text: In 1926, Erwin Schrödinger wrote an equation that describes electrons as waves, not particles in fixed orbits.

discovery.text: The wave function gives the probability of finding an electron at any point. Dense cloud means high probability.

twist.text: Electron orbitals have bizarre shapes: spheres (s), dumbbells (p), clovers (d), and complex flowers (f).

climax.text: These orbital shapes determine how atoms bond. All of chemistry flows from the geometry of electron clouds.

punchline.text: Not orbits but clouds. Probability replaces certainty. Chemistry got fuzzy.

quiz.question: What does Schrödinger's equation tell us about electrons?
options: The probability of finding an electron at any given point in space
The exact position and speed of each electron
How fast electrons orbit the nucleus

hook.text: You cannot know both the exact position and momentum of an electron at the same time. Nature forbids it.

buildup.text: Werner Heisenberg showed in 1927 that measuring one property precisely makes the other fundamentally uncertain.

discovery.text: This isn't about bad instruments. The uncertainty is built into nature—a particle simply doesn't have both at once.

twist.text: Einstein hated this. He said 'God does not play dice.' Bohr replied: 'Stop telling God what to do.'

climax.text: This principle explains atomic stability. Electrons can't collapse into the nucleus—that would fix their position too precisely.

punchline.text: Nature has a built-in blur. Perfect knowledge is impossible at the atomic scale.

quiz.question: What does Heisenberg's uncertainty principle state?
options: You cannot simultaneously know both the exact position and momentum of a particle
Electrons move too fast to observe accurately
All measurements have errors due to instrument limitations

topics.title: Electron Spin: The Fourth Quantum Number

hook.text: Electrons spin—but not like a top. Quantum spin is an intrinsic property with only two values: up or down.

buildup.text: In 1925, Uhlenbeck and Goudsmit proposed electron spin to explain why spectral lines split in magnetic fields.

discovery.text: Spin gives electrons a tiny magnetic moment. Each orbital holds exactly two electrons—one spin-up, one spin-down.

twist.text: The Pauli exclusion principle says no two electrons can share the same quantum numbers. Spin doubles orbital capacity.

climax.text: Without spin, atoms would be flat. The periodic table, chemical bonds, and all matter depend on this quantum property.

punchline.text: Up or down, nothing between. A binary property that shapes all of chemistry.

quiz.question: Why is electron spin important for chemistry?
options: It doubles orbital capacity and determines the structure of the periodic table
It creates the force that holds atoms together
It determines the color of each element

hook.text: Electrons behave as particles when you detect them and as waves when you don't. They're both and neither.

buildup.text: In 1924, Louis de Broglie proposed that all matter has wave properties. The smaller the particle, the larger the wave.

discovery.text: The double-slit experiment proved it: electrons fired one at a time build up an interference pattern—like waves.

twist.text: But if you watch which slit each electron goes through, the wave pattern vanishes. Observation changes behavior.

climax.text: This duality is fundamental. Electron microscopes work because electron wavelengths are smaller than light—revealing atoms.

punchline.text: Particle or wave depends on how you look. Reality is stranger than intuition.

quiz.question: What happens when you observe which slit an electron passes through?
options: The wave interference pattern vanishes and electrons behave like particles
The electron splits into two halves
Nothing changes—the interference pattern remains

hook.text: Table salt forms when sodium gives an electron to chlorine. The charged atoms attract and lock into a crystal.

buildup.text: Sodium has one outer electron it wants to lose. Chlorine needs one more electron to fill its outer shell.

discovery.text: Sodium becomes Na⁺ and chlorine becomes Cl⁻. The opposite charges attract with enormous force—an ionic bond.

twist.text: Ionic compounds form rigid crystal lattices, not molecules. Each Na⁺ is surrounded by six Cl⁻ ions and vice versa.

climax.text: Dissolve salt in water and the ions separate. That's why salt water conducts electricity—free ions carry the current.

punchline.text: Give an electron, take an electron, opposite charges lock together. That's salt.

quiz.question: Why does salt dissolve in water and conduct electricity?
options: Water separates Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions, which carry electrical current
Salt molecules break apart into individual atoms
Water chemically reacts with salt to produce electrons

hook.text: Two hydrogen atoms share their electrons to form H₂. Neither gains nor loses—they share. That's a covalent bond.

buildup.text: Each hydrogen has one electron but wants two to fill its shell. Sharing gives both atoms a full outer shell.

discovery.text: The shared electrons create a region of negative charge between the nuclei, pulling them together.

twist.text: Double bonds share four electrons (like O₂). Triple bonds share six (like N₂). More sharing means shorter, stronger bonds.

climax.text: Diamond is pure carbon with four covalent bonds per atom. This 3D network makes it the hardest natural material.

punchline.text: Sharing electrons, both atoms benefit. Cooperation at the atomic level.

quiz.question: What makes diamond so hard?
options: Every carbon atom forms four covalent bonds in a rigid 3D network
Diamond contains metallic bonds between carbon atoms
Diamond's atoms are compressed by extreme pressure underground

hook.text: Metals conduct electricity because their outer electrons don't belong to any one atom—they flow freely like a sea.

buildup.text: Metal atoms release outer electrons into a shared pool. Positive ion cores sit in this 'electron sea.'

discovery.text: Free electrons carry charge (conductivity), transmit heat (thermal conductivity), and reflect light (metallic shine).

twist.text: Metals are malleable because ion layers can slide past each other without breaking bonds—the electron sea flows with them.

climax.text: Alloys mix different metal atoms, disrupting the regular lattice. This makes them harder—steel is stronger than pure iron.

punchline.text: Electrons free to roam. That sea of charge is why metals conduct, shine, and bend.

quiz.question: Why are metals malleable (able to be hammered into shape)?
options: Ion layers slide past each other while the electron sea maintains the bond
Metal atoms are very small and can move easily
Heat from hammering melts the metal slightly

hook.text: Water shouldn't be liquid at room temperature. It's too small a molecule. Hydrogen bonds are why it is.

buildup.text: Oxygen pulls electron density away from hydrogen, creating a partial negative charge on O and partial positive on H.

discovery.text: The positive H of one water molecule attracts the negative O of another. This weak bond forms and breaks constantly.

twist.text: Without hydrogen bonds, water would boil at -80°C—like hydrogen sulfide. Earth would have no liquid water, and no life.

climax.text: DNA's double helix is held together by hydrogen bonds between base pairs. They're strong enough to hold, weak enough to unzip.

punchline.text: A weak bond that makes water liquid and holds DNA together. Life's essential glue.

quiz.question: Why is water liquid at room temperature despite being a very small molecule?
options: Hydrogen bonds between water molecules raise the boiling point dramatically
Oxygen atoms are very heavy, keeping molecules close
Water molecules are magnetic and attract each other

topics.title: Van der Waals Forces: The Weakest Bonds

hook.text: Geckos climb glass walls using Van der Waals forces—the weakest attraction between atoms, but billions at once.

buildup.text: Even nonpolar molecules have fleeting charge imbalances as electrons move. These create temporary tiny dipoles.

discovery.text: A temporary dipole in one molecule induces an opposite dipole in its neighbor. They briefly attract—then shift again.

twist.text: These forces are incredibly weak alone. But gecko toe pads have billions of tiny hairs, each adding Van der Waals pull.

climax.text: Van der Waals forces explain why noble gases can be liquefied at extreme cold—even with no chemical bonds.

punchline.text: Fleeting, tiny, everywhere. The weakest force holds geckos to ceilings.

quiz.question: How do geckos climb smooth glass surfaces?
options: Billions of tiny hairs generate enough Van der Waals force to support their weight
They secrete a sticky adhesive from their feet
Their feet create a vacuum seal against the surface

topics.title: Radioactive Decay: Atoms That Fall Apart

hook.text: Some atoms are unstable. They spontaneously emit particles and energy to reach a more stable state—that's radioactive decay.

buildup.text: Henri Becquerel discovered radioactivity in 1896 when uranium salts fogged a photographic plate in the dark.

discovery.text: Three types of decay: alpha (emits a helium nucleus), beta (emits an electron), and gamma (emits pure energy).

twist.text: Each isotope has a unique half-life—the time for half the atoms to decay. It ranges from microseconds to billions of years.

climax.text: Uranium-238 decays through 14 steps over 4.5 billion years before finally becoming stable lead-206.

punchline.text: Unstable atoms shed particles until they find peace. Nature's slow countdown.

quiz.question: What are the three types of radioactive decay?
options: Alpha (helium nucleus), beta (electron), and gamma (energy)
Fission, fusion, and annihilation
Proton emission, neutron emission, and electron capture

hook.text: In 1938, Otto Hahn split a uranium atom and released a million times more energy than any chemical reaction.

buildup.text: A neutron strikes uranium-235, making it unstable. The nucleus splits into two smaller atoms plus 2–3 neutrons.

discovery.text: Those freed neutrons hit more uranium atoms, causing a chain reaction. Each split releases enormous energy from E = mc².

twist.text: Lise Meitner actually explained the physics of fission. Hahn got the Nobel Prize alone—one of science's greatest injustices.

climax.text: Controlled fission powers nuclear reactors. Uncontrolled fission is an atomic bomb. Same reaction, different speeds.

punchline.text: Split one atom, free neutrons split more. A chain reaction of pure energy.

quiz.question: What sustains a nuclear fission chain reaction?
options: Neutrons released from each split strike more atoms, causing further splits
Heat from the first split melts surrounding atoms
Electrons escaping from split atoms trigger nearby nuclei

hook.text: The Sun fuses 600 million tons of hydrogen into helium every second. Fusion is the energy source of every star.

buildup.text: At 15 million degrees, hydrogen nuclei move fast enough to overcome electromagnetic repulsion and fuse together.

discovery.text: Four hydrogen nuclei fuse into one helium nucleus. The helium weighs less—the missing mass becomes pure energy via E = mc².

twist.text: Fusion releases 4 times more energy per gram than fission. But we still can't sustain it on Earth—containment is the problem.

climax.text: ITER in France is building a reactor that uses magnetic fields to contain 150-million-degree plasma. First tests aim for 2035.

punchline.text: Smash light atoms together, lose mass, gain energy. The Sun's recipe for power.

quiz.question: Why is nuclear fusion so difficult to achieve on Earth?
options: It requires extreme temperatures and we can't contain the plasma effectively
The fuel (hydrogen) is too rare on Earth
Fusion reactions produce too much radiation to be safe

hook.text: Every living thing absorbs Carbon-14 from the atmosphere. When it dies, the C-14 starts ticking away like a clock.

buildup.text: Cosmic rays hit nitrogen in the atmosphere, creating C-14. Plants absorb it as CO₂; animals eat the plants.

discovery.text: C-14 decays with a half-life of 5,730 years. Measure how much remains and you know when the organism died.

twist.text: Willard Libby invented the technique in 1949 and won the Nobel Prize. It revolutionized archaeology overnight.

climax.text: C-14 dating proved the Dead Sea Scrolls were 2,000 years old and the Shroud of Turin was medieval, not ancient.

punchline.text: Atoms decay at a known rate. Death starts the clock. Chemistry reads it.

quiz.question: What is the half-life of Carbon-14?
options: 5,730 years
57,300 years
573 years

hook.text: For every particle, there's an antiparticle—identical but with opposite charge. When they meet, both annihilate into pure energy.

buildup.text: Paul Dirac predicted antimatter in 1928. Carl Anderson found the positron (anti-electron) in cosmic rays in 1932.

discovery.text: When an electron meets a positron, their combined mass converts entirely to energy: two gamma rays flying apart.

twist.text: PET scans use antimatter. Radioactive tracers emit positrons that annihilate with electrons, producing detectable gamma rays.

climax.text: The Big Bang should have created equal matter and antimatter. Why matter survived is one of physics' biggest mysteries.

punchline.text: Mirror particles that destroy on contact. The universe's most explosive chemistry.

quiz.question: What happens when matter meets antimatter?
options: Both annihilate completely, converting all mass into pure energy
They repel each other like magnets
The antimatter passes through normal matter unchanged
